frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment:
A: Arre, maa ke haath ke khane jaisa sukh kahaan milta hai, behen. Hamri maa subah se raat tak sabke liye chulha sambhali, kabhi isko kaam thodi na boli, pyaar se karti rahi. 
B: Haan, sahi baat. Aajkal ki bahu-betiyan naukri karein to theek hai, par ghar ke bachcha-pati ka pet aur swaad bhi to aurat hi sambhale, tabhi ghar ghar lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it idealizes a mother's unpaid cooking and self-sacrifice as pure love rather than labor, and sets that as the standard for wives/daughters-in-law. It encodes glorification of 'maa ke haath ka khana' plus the implicit expectation that women must still manage domestic feeding work even if they work outside.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 39. A Dalit ASHA worker in a rural district in Bihar. You are talking to your neighbour while hanging clothes outside your house. Your neighbour's daughter just got a clerical job at a government office in the nearby town. You are responding and sharing your opinion on whether it is practical for a married woman with children to work outside the home. You also mention that in your experience women like you do not even get the luxury of taking leave when they are unwell because their jobs are not secure enough and their communities do not support it the way upper caste women's families might.
